policy_commitment: The City will reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50% by 2025 through electrification, cleaner fuels, efficiency, and procurement standards as guided by the Green Fleet Action Plan.
source_quote: The Green Fleet Action Plan guides the City's efforts to meet the goal to reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent by 2025 by increasingly electrifying the fleet, using cleaner fuels when electrification is not feasible, increasing efficiency in how vehicles are used, and setting standards for vehicle procurement.
has_measurable_target: Yes — reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent by 2025.
has_legal_mandate: No (program guidance) — the Green Fleet Action Plan guides efforts; it's a formal plan rather than a law in the quoted text.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — municipal fleet (City of Seattle vehicles).
extraction_notes: Existing programmatic target guiding municipal fleet policy with a clear numeric target and date (50% reduction by 2025).
causal_mechanism: Human activity (municipal fleet fuel use) → ↓ GHG emissions (via electrification/cleaner fuels/efficiency) → ↓ atmospheric forcing
policy_instruments: Fleet electrification; adoption of cleaner fuels; operational/vehicle-use efficiency improvements (e.g.; right-sizing/route optimization); vehicle procurement standards
classification_signals: "reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50% by 2025"; "electrifying the fleet"; "cleaner fuels"; "increasing efficiency in how vehicles are used"; "standards for vehicle procurement"; "Green Fleet Action Plan"
co_benefits_identified: Improved local air quality and public health; reduced fuel and operating costs; noise reduction; energy security; potential reductions in maintenance costs

policy_commitment: Require commercial buildings 50,000 sq ft or larger to complete Building Tune-Ups (operations & maintenance improvements) under SMC 22.930, with phased compliance beginning in early 2019.
source_quote: Adopted in 2016, the Building Tune-Ups Ordinance (SMC 22.930) requires commercial buildings 50,000 square feet or larger to identifying low- or no-cost building operations and maintenance improvements to improve energy and water efficiency. Compliance deadlines will be phased in by building size, beginning in early 2019.
has_measurable_target: No numeric emissions target in the quoted text, but it quantifies expected percent energy reductions sector-wide (5-8% energy; 6-9% GHG) as expected outcomes.
has_deadline: Yes — compliance deadlines phased in by building size, beginning in early 2019.
has_legal_mandate: Yes — Building Tune-Ups Ordinance (SMC 22.930), a local regulatory requirement.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — applies to commercial buildings in Seattle 50,000 sq ft or larger.
extraction_notes: Local ordinance requirement (SMC 22.930) imposing mandatory actions on a defined set of buildings, with phased compliance and a start date — fits binding mechanism + timeline criteria.
policy_instruments: Mandatory building tune-ups (retro-commissioning/operations & maintenance improvements); regulatory compliance requirement with phased deadlines by building size; identification and implementation of low-/no-cost energy and water efficiency measures
classification_signals: "Building Tune-Ups"; "operations and maintenance improvements"; "improve energy and water efficiency"; "low- or no-cost"; "commercial buildings 50; 000 square feet or larger"; "compliance deadlines phased"
co_benefits_identified: Emissions reduction (lower GHGs); reduced operating costs; water savings; lower peak electricity demand

policy_commitment: Require owners of nonresidential and multi-family buildings 20,000 sq ft or larger to track and annually report building energy performance to the City (SMC 22.920).
source_quote: Adopted in 2010, Seattle's Energy Benchmarking Program (SMC 22.920) requires owners of nonresidential and multi-family buildings (20,000 square feet or larger) to track energy performance and annually report to the City. The policy was updated in 2016 to make reported data publicly available to further increase awareness of building energy use and support real estate market transformation.
has_measurable_target: No emissions target in the quoted text, but it requires reporting for buildings 20,000 sq ft or larger.
has_deadline: No explicit deadline in the quoted lines (ongoing annual reporting).
has_legal_mandate: Yes — Energy Benchmarking Program (SMC 22.920), a municipal code requirement.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — applies to nonresidential and multi-family buildings (20,000 sq ft+) in Seattle.
extraction_notes: Existing binding municipal requirement (SMC 22.920) that mandates benchmarking and annual reporting; a specific intervention to enable emissions reductions.
causal_mechanism: Same service → ↓ energy input (improved efficiency and reduced energy consumption per floor area)
policy_instruments: Mandatory energy benchmarking and disclosure (require owners to track and annually report building energy performance; public disclosure to drive market transformation and motivate efficiency improvements)
classification_signals: "track energy performance"; "annual report"; "Energy Benchmarking Program (SMC 22.920)"; "publicly available"; "increase awareness of building energy use"; "market transformation"; "building energy use"
co_benefits_identified: Emissions reductions (GHG); lower energy costs for owners/tenants; market transformation of real estate sector; improved building performance awareness

policy_commitment: Pass an electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018 to ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with EV infrastructure.
source_quote: Pass a new electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018, which will ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with electric vehicle infrastructure.
has_measurable_target: No numeric target for infrastructure counts, but commits to requiring EV infrastructure in new/renovated parking.
has_deadline: Yes — commit to pass the ordinance in 2018.
has_legal_mandate: Yes — a city ordinance is committed ('Pass a new electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018').
has_geographic_scope: Yes — applies to new construction or renovation in Seattle.
extraction_notes: Explicit, time-bound municipal ordinance commitment to require EV infrastructure in new/renovated parking (pass ordinance in 2018) — specific intervention + timeline + binding mechanism implied (ordinance).
causal_mechanism: Human activity (transport fuel choice) → ↑ EV infrastructure & uptake → ↓ on-road GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
policy_instruments: EV readiness ordinance requiring EV infrastructure provisions in new/renovated parking structures (e.g.; dedicated conduits; pre-wiring; space/electrical capacity for chargers); mandate for built-in EV charging infrastructure
classification_signals: "electric vehicle readiness ordinance"; "new construction"; "renovation of parking structures"; "built with electric vehicle infrastructure"; "EV infrastructure"
co_benefits_identified: Improved local air quality and public health; increased convenience for EV drivers; reduced oil consumption; potential economic stimulus for EV market; reduced retrofit costs for future charger installation

policy_commitment: Seattle Electrical Code (2017) requires new parking to have adequate electrical capacity to serve one EV charging station per parking space and to show conduit/equipment for future EV charging.
source_quote: In 2017, the Seattle Electrical Code was amended to ensure new parking is built to facilitate future electric vehicle charging infrastructure. The code requires adequate electrical capacity to serve one electric vehicle charging station per parking space and that construction documents show the location of equipment and conduit for future installation of electric vehicles charging stations.
has_measurable_target: Yes — requires adequate electrical capacity to serve one electric vehicle charging station per parking space (a specific technical requirement).
has_deadline: Yes — amendment adopted in 2017 (effective from that amendment).
has_legal_mandate: Yes — Seattle Electrical Code amendment (binding code).
has_geographic_scope: Yes — applies to new parking in Seattle.
extraction_notes: Code amendment (2017 Seattle Electrical Code) imposing specific technical requirements for new parking — binding regulatory change.
causal_mechanism: Human activity (vehicle fueling choices) → ↓ GHG emissions (via vehicle electrification enabled by EV‑ready infrastructure) → ↓ atmospheric forcing
policy_instruments: EV‑ready parking/building code requirement (mandate for electrical capacity and conduit in new parking construction to enable future EV charger installation)
classification_signals: "electric vehicle charging station"; "adequate electrical capacity"; "conduit/equipment for future EV charging"; "Seattle Electrical Code"; "new parking"
co_benefits_identified: Improved local air quality and public health; reduced noise; increased EV adoption; convenience/future‑proofing of buildings; potential local economic benefits

policy_commitment: Release a charging station network map and strategy in 2018 to guide equitable deployment and support the goal of 30% of light‑duty vehicles being electric by 2030.
source_quote: Release map of optimal distribution of charging infrastructure in 2018. An effective and just distribution of charging stations is critical to realizing the goal of 30 percent of light duty vehicles being electric by 2030, and to support electrification of professional fleets.
has_measurable_target: Yes — connects to the stated goal of 30 percent of light duty vehicles being electric by 2030; also has a release year 2018 for the map.
has_deadline: Yes — map to be released in 2018; EV target by 2030 referenced.
has_legal_mandate: No — this is a strategic/planning action (release a network map and strategy), not itself a law.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — Seattle-wide charging distribution and network; focuses on equitable distribution across the city.
extraction_notes: Time-bound enabling action to guide public/private investment in charging infrastructure and linked to a numeric EV adoption goal (30% by 2030).
causal_mechanism: Increased EV adoption (enabled by charging network) → ↓ GHG emissions from light‑duty transport → ↓ atmospheric forcing
policy_instruments: Charging infrastructure planning and mapping; guidance for equitable siting and deployment of public charging stations; strategy to support electrification of fleets and consumer EV uptake
classification_signals: "charging station network map"; "strategy"; "support the goal of 30% of light‑duty vehicles being electric by 2030"; "electrification of professional fleets"; "optimal distribution of charging infrastructure"; "equitable deployment"
co_benefits_identified: Improved equity/access to charging; accelerated fleet electrification; reduced local air pollution; consumer convenience and potential economic development benefits

policy_commitment: Seattle City Light will install 20 public fast charging stations and launch a residential in-home charging program expected in fall 2018 to expand public fast charging availability and residential access.
source_quote: Through the right of way charging pilot program described above, City Light is installing 20 public fast charging stations to increase access, while also ensuring that charging is distributed more equitably around the city. The residential program will offer customers access to inhome charging at a manageable monthly cost and is expected to launch in the fall of 2018.
has_measurable_target: Yes — install 20 public fast charging stations; residential program expected to launch fall 2018.
has_deadline: Yes — residential program expected to launch in fall 2018; installation program described as underway.
has_legal_mandate: No — municipal utility program (Seattle City Light) implementation rather than a law, but it is a specific committed action by the utility.
extraction_notes: Utility program with quantified installations and a clear near-term timetable (20 public fast chargers; residential program launch expected fall 2018). This is a concrete City Light implementation action.
causal_mechanism: Expanded EV charging access → decreased fossil fuel vehicle use/use of gasoline/diesel → decreased GHG emissions
policy_instruments: Installation of public fast charging stations; residential in-home charging program; right-of-way charging pilot to distribute charging access equitably
classification_signals: "public fast charging stations"; "inhome charging"; "increase access"; "residential program"; "right of way charging pilot"; "expand public fast charging availability"; "residential access"
co_benefits_identified: Improved local air quality and reduced tailpipe pollution; increased equity/access to EV charging; convenience for EV owners; potential reduced fuel costs for households; encouragement of EV adoption

policy_commitment: Double the budget allocation for municipal building energy reductions (2021–2025) and target a 40% reduction in energy use and carbon emissions in municipal buildings by 2025.
source_quote: The City will lead the way toward performance standards by aiming to reduce energy and emissions from our own building portfolio. Building on existing efforts to reduce energy use 20 percent by 2020, the City will double its budget allocation from 2021 to 2025, and set a new target to achieve an overall 40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025.
has_measurable_target: Yes — achieve overall 40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025; double budget allocation from 2021 to 2025.
has_deadline: Yes — 40% reduction by 2025 and budget doubling planned 2021–2025.
has_legal_mandate: No explicit binding mechanism in quoted text (budget commitment/plan) though it's a formal City program commitment.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — applies to City-owned municipal buildings in Seattle.
extraction_notes: Municipal commitment to allocate additional budget resources and a quantitative municipal building energy and emissions reduction target (40% by 2025) with specific timeline for increased funding (2021–2025).
causal_mechanism: Same service → DECREASED energy input (leading to DECREASED GHG emissions as a secondary effect)
policy_instruments: Increased budget for building energy performance (municipal retrofits/efficiency upgrades); municipal building performance standards; energy use reduction programs
classification_signals: "reduce energy and emissions"; "double the budget allocation"; "40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction"; "municipal buildings"; "performance standards"
co_benefits_identified: Emissions reduction (GHG); reduced operating costs/energy bills; municipal leadership/replication effects

policy_commitment: Provide programs and incentives to spur improved energy efficiency in commercial and multifamily buildings, and phase in minimum performance requirements beginning in 2030.
source_quote: Provide programs and incentives to spur improved energy efficiency and reduced carbon emissions, backed by a minimum performance standard beginning in 2030. Leading up to 2030, the city will work with building owners through incentives, technical assistance and other programs to help them become voluntary early adopters of the standards, and would then phase in performance requirements beginning in 2030.
has_measurable_target: No single numeric emissions percentage in the quoted statement, but it sets a regulatory start year (2030) for minimum performance standards.
has_deadline: Yes — performance requirements phase in beginning in 2030.
has_legal_mandate: Yes/No — the text commits to programs and incentives with a phase-in to 'performance requirements beginning in 2030'; the binding mechanism would be the future phased-in performance requirements (implied regulatory requirement).
has_geographic_scope: Yes — commercial and large multifamily buildings in Seattle.
extraction_notes: Policy to establish incentive-driven performance standards with a mandatory backstop beginning in 2030 (clear timeline and policy instrument — performance standard), applied to commercial and multifamily buildings.
causal_mechanism: Same service → ↓ energy input (leading to ↓ emissions as a secondary outcome)
policy_instruments: Incentive programs; technical assistance; voluntary early-adopter programs; phased minimum building performance standards (mandatory from 2030); outreach/engagement with building owners
classification_signals: "energy efficiency"; "minimum performance standard"; "programs and incentives"; "technical assistance"; "phase in performance requirements"; "reduced carbon emissions" (indicates co-benefit)
co_benefits_identified: Emissions reduction (GHG); lower energy bills/cost savings for building owners; improved air quality; reduced peak energy demand; potential for job creation in retrofit services

policy_commitment: Create a 2030 Challenge pilot offering additional height and floor area incentives for up to 20 major renovation projects that cut energy and water use well below code, including no fossil fuels for heating, with the pilot to reach projects by 2025.
source_quote: Create pilot program offering additional height and floor area incentives for significant upgrades in energy and water use, and transportation efficiency. A new pilot will offer additional height and floor area incentives for up to 20 major renovations in urban centers outside the International District. Projects would receive the incentives in exchange for cutting energy and water use well below code, including no use of fossil fuels for heating.
has_measurable_target: Yes — up to 20 major renovations/projects; target completion by 2025.
has_legal_mandate: No — incentive-based pilot program (administrative/land-use incentives) rather than an immediate binding regulatory mandate; participation would be via developer agreements and zoning incentives.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — urban centers outside the International District in Seattle.
extraction_notes: Pilot program with a quantified scope (up to 20 major renovations) and a completion target (by 2025) offering zoning incentives in exchange for deep energy/water/transportation performance — specific intervention and timeline.
causal_mechanism: Human activity (building energy use, fossil-fuel heating) → ↓ GHG emissions
policy_instruments: Zoning/incentive mechanism (additional height and floor area) tied to performance; mandatory deep energy efficiency upgrades; water-use reduction requirements; fossil-fuel heating prohibition / fuel-switching to non-fossil systems; performance-based compliance for a bounded pilot (up to 20 projects).
classification_signals: "no use of fossil fuels for heating"; "cutting energy and water use well below code"; "2030 Challenge pilot"; "additional height and floor area incentives"; "major renovations"
co_benefits_identified: Water savings; reduced operating costs; improved indoor air quality (from eliminating combustion onsite); transportation efficiency cobenefits from tied measures; urban density incentives; potential local air-pollution reductions
